steps.title: Summarize each author's view
steps.content: Text 1 says it "is best" to understand Soyinka's drama chronologically and divides it into three periods, each with "a particular thematic and stylistic cohesion." Text 2 calls it "tempting" to impose such order but argues critics who use three phases "overlook potential commonalities" across phases and "discount significant differences" within them.

steps.title: Name the difference
steps.content: Text 1 endorses the three-phase framework as useful; Text 2 treats it as a limiting lens that hides cross-phase similarities and within-phase variation.

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — While the author of Text 1 believes that thinking about Soyinka's works of theater in phases is useful, the author of Text 2 views such an approach as limiting.: matches both authors' positions exactly. ✓
  • Choice B — Although the author of Text 1 claims that Soyinka's style as a dramatist has evolved over time, the author of Text 2 argues that Soyinka's style has remained consistent throughout his career.: Text 2 never claims consistency; it claims phases miss both commonalities and differences — misreads Text 2. ✗
  • Choice C — The author of Text 1 considers Soyinka's plays to showcase his strongest writing, whereas the author of Text 2 believes that Soyinka's poetry is where he is most skilled.: neither text discusses poetry — overreach beyond what is stated. ✗
  • Choice D — The author of Text 1 argues that Soyinka's early plays were his most politically charged, whereas the author of Text 2 claims that Soyinka's most recent plays are the most politicized.: neither text ranks plays by political content — overreach. ✗
A is correct.
